<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: capability-replacement-and-rollback
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/capability-replacement-and-rollback.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/capability-replacement-and-rollback.qmd@26297f08a
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Capability Replacement and Rollback

Stable Capability Fields define what must remain true when a capability
changes. Capability Replacement and Rollback defines how the change is allowed
to happen.

An upgrade is not a moral event, and it is not a magic word. A new component
may be faster, cleaner, cheaper, or more capable while still weakening the
system. It may erase an old regression, widen a route's authority, change the
meaning of a field, make state migration insolvent, or approve itself through
an evaluator it helped shape. If that happens, the system has not improved. It
has traded visible progress for hidden drift.

Replacement is the stack's way of refusing that trade. A candidate
implementation enters as a transaction, not as a hope. The transaction records
the prior artifact, the candidate artifact, the field identity, the evidence
packet, the regression floor, the authority check, the evaluator boundary, the
canary scope, the monitor window, the residuals, and the rollback receipt. Only
then can improvement become ordinary infrastructure.

The core discipline is reversible progress. A self-improving system should be
trusted only to the degree that failure can be detected, contained, explained,
and undone.

## Problem

Recursive improvement creates a steady stream of proposed replacements. A tool
becomes a compiled procedure. A specialist route becomes cheaper. A verifier
gets stricter. A benchmark strategy changes. A policy update improves the
frontier. Each proposal may be useful, but each proposal also creates a risk:
the system may forget the floor while chasing the frontier.

The floor includes more than accuracy. It includes field identity, regression
history, permission scope, auditability, context restrictions, human review,
residual ownership, and recovery paths. A candidate that wins a new comparison
while losing those older obligations should not become default.

Capability replacement therefore needs a procedure. Stable Capability Fields
provide the target identity. The replacement transaction decides whether a
candidate has earned promotion without moving that identity, losing old
evidence, or spending more authority than it was granted.

## Why Ordinary Upgrades Are Not Enough

Ordinary upgrades are often one-way stories. The new version wins a test, ships
to production, and the organization learns about the missing regression only
after dependent work has already moved forward. That pattern is risky in
ordinary software. It is worse in a self-improving AI stack, where the candidate
may also affect the evidence, evaluator, route, or authority surface that
judges it.

Ad hoc replacement cannot answer the questions that matter:

- What exact field is being preserved?
- Which prior artifact and state are recoverable?
- Which regressions moved forward?
- Which authority changed?
- Who or what evaluated the candidate?
- What failed in canary?
- What residuals remain?
- What triggers rollback, and who owns it?

Corrigibility is the external baseline for why this matters. Soares et al.
(2015) frame correction and intervention as properties that can be lost by
design. A replacement transaction is the systems-engineering version of that
concern: it asks whether correction channels, regression floors, evaluator
separation, and recovery handles survived the upgrade. This repository uses
that paper as comparator and vocabulary, not as proof that the ASI Stack
implements corrigibility.

The essential distinction is candidate improvement versus accepted
replacement. A candidate can be promising in shadow mode. It can win a canary.
It can lower cost or improve a benchmark slice. Accepted replacement means
something stronger: the stack has preserved field identity, checked authority
boundaries, retained regression floors, recorded residuals, kept a monitor
window, and maintained a recovery path.

## Core Claim

Capability replacement should be an evidence-gated transaction with
preconditions, regression checks, residual records, and rollback. In the live
book, this claim remains a design rationale supported at `argument`.

The source support is architectural. SCF supplies field identity,
implementation-artifact, qualification, state-migration, lifecycle,
evaluator-integrity, and rollback vocabulary. RMI supplies the ratchet idea:
move the frontier while preserving the floor through residual escrow,
critical-failure vetoes, and specialist lifecycle discipline. Benchmaxxing
supplies benchmark lifecycle, wall diagnosis, anti-Goodhart safeguards,
regression promotion, and residual-ledger discipline. Cognitive Loop Closure
supplies verified tool/procedure lifecycle, monitoring, revision, and
retirement pressure. Talos supplies audit, replay, job, proof-bundle, and human
adjudication context. MoECOT remains runtime-reference context only.

None of those sources is treated here as a reproduced replacement run. The
current evidence does not prove deployed replacement behavior, real regression
suite coverage, monitor-window success, evaluator-integrity enforcement,
authority enforcement, rollback execution, benchmark improvement, or MoECOT
runtime behavior.

## Mechanism

A replacement transaction turns a proposed improvement into a controlled state
change. It is allowed to fail. It is not allowed to fail invisibly.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Prior["Prior implementation"] --> Proposal["Replacement proposal"]
  Proposal --> Evidence["Wall diagnosis + evidence packet"]
  Evidence --> Precheck["Precheck: field identity + authority"]
  Precheck --> Gates["Qualification gates"]
  Gates --> Floor["Regression floor + holdouts"]
  Floor --> Residual["Residual escrow"]
  Residual --> Ready{"Rollback ready?"}
  Ready -- "yes" --> Canary["Canary / commit candidate"]
  Ready -- "no" --> Reject["Reject / quarantine / residual"]
  Canary --> Monitor["Monitor window"]
  Monitor --> Incident{"Incident or regression?"}
  Incident -- "yes" --> Rollback["Rollback / compensate / fork"]
  Incident -- "no" --> Promote["Promote or retain as default"]
  Reject --> Ledger["Evidence + changelog"]
  Rollback --> Ledger
  Promote --> Ledger
```

The first object is the proposal. It names the prior implementation, candidate
implementation, field, motivation, evidence packet, authority delta, and
claimed identity preservation.

The second object is the gate set. The transaction checks qualification
evidence, regression floors, authority boundaries, evaluator independence,
state migration, canary scope, and readiness. A candidate may supply evidence,
but it cannot be the sole evaluator, gatekeeper, or rollback authority for its
own promotion.

The third object is the residual record. Failed gates, missing regressions,
state-migration uncertainty, weak transfer, benchmark saturation, and
unresolved side effects must become residuals. They should not disappear behind
a successful upgrade story.

The fourth object is the rollback receipt. Rollback is not nostalgia for the
old version. It needs the prior artifact, state migration status, reversible
fields, irreversible effects, dry-run status, trigger conditions, and owner. If
the old state cannot be interpreted, or if external effects remain unresolved,
the record should say compensation, fork, quarantine, or residual rather than
pretending full recovery.

Benchmarks enter as evidence surfaces, not permission slips. A benchmark result
can help explain why a candidate exists. It cannot by itself prove that the
candidate preserved identity, evaluator independence, authority boundaries, or
rollback readiness.

## A Replacement Scenario

Suppose a planner in the stack has a slower but trusted implementation and a
new candidate that produces shorter plans on a benchmark slice. An ordinary
upgrade story would stop there: the candidate is better, so it ships. A
replacement transaction asks a different set of questions before the word
better is allowed to matter.

The record names the stable capability field first. The candidate must show
that it is still the same kind of planner, with the same authority ceiling,
same human-approval obligations, same context restrictions, and same audit
retention. The old regression suite follows the field forward. If the new
planner wins on short planning tasks but forgets a denial path for tool
authority, that failure becomes a blocker, not a tradeoff hidden inside an
average score.

The candidate then enters shadow or canary scope. It may plan for a narrow
workload, but the prior implementation remains available. The monitor window
has explicit triggers: missed approval, unowned external effect, larger hidden
context request, failed regression, stale evidence, or cost breach. The rollback
receipt names the old artifact, the state migration boundary, the dry-run
status, and the owner who must act if the monitor fires. If the candidate has
already caused irreversible external effects, the record says compensation,
fork, quarantine, or residual; it does not pretend rollback is clean.

This scenario does not claim a deployed planner replacement, production
rollback, real monitor quality, evaluator-integrity enforcement, or benchmark
improvement. It gives the reader the shape of a valid replacement: the
candidate may be promising, but default status belongs only to the transaction
that preserved the floor.

## Interfaces

Replacement connects the stack's ledgers.

The SCF ledger says which capability identity must survive. The benchmark
ledger says which frontier and regression evidence was used. The artifact graph
says which prior, candidate, state-migration, and rollback artifacts exist. The
changelog says what became default, what stayed canary, what was rejected, and
which residuals remain.

A minimum replacement record needs a transaction ID, transaction state, field
ID, prior implementation, candidate implementation, identity-preservation
statement, precheck results, qualification evidence, regression results,
authority check, evaluator-independence statement, residual escrow, rollback
plan, rollback receipt, approval record, canary scope, monitor window, monitor
status, decision, promotion blockers, source references, support-state effect,
and non-claims.

Those fields let other layers inspect the same event. The security layer can
ask whether the candidate crosses a secret or permission boundary. Runtime
adapters can ask whether effect authority changed. Governance can ask whether
human review, appeal, exit, or audit paths were weakened. Policy optimization
can ask whether learning pressure is trying to ratify its own evaluator. The
transaction gives every layer a concrete artifact instead of asking them to
trust the word "upgrade."

Transaction states should be explicit: proposed, precheck, shadow, canary,
default-candidate, committed, rolled-back, quarantined, superseded, retired,
and blocked. A candidate that survived one test should not become default
everywhere by inertia.

## Invariants

No replacement should happen without prior and posterior artifacts. A later
reviewer must be able to see what changed, why it was allowed, what got worse,
and how recovery was supposed to work.

Regressions stay attached. The new candidate inherits the field's old
obligations, including boring ones: cost ceilings, permission limits, logging,
context restrictions, audit retention, human review behavior, and export or
fork rights.

Rollback metadata is required before default promotion. The receipt must name
reversible fields, irreversible effects, trigger conditions, and owner.

Evaluator independence protects the gate. A candidate may generate evidence,
but it cannot be the only judge of whether that evidence is enough.

Scope stability protects against quiet authority expansion. A candidate can be
accepted for one field, workload, permission tier, or context boundary without
becoming default everywhere.

The current Lean hooks cover small finite-record versions of two boundaries:
a modeled replacement commit requires qualification evidence and rollback
metadata, and a failed regression blocks promotion in the model. Those hooks are
useful, but they do not prove deployed replacement execution, real regression
suite coverage, monitor-window behavior, or rollback execution.

There is also now a capability replacement trace probe. It uses a baseline
implementation, a candidate kept in canary, a synthetic monitor trigger, a
rollback dry run, and residual records. Three bad controls are rejected:
authority widening, failed regression, and missing rollback. That makes the
transaction shape easier to inspect, but it is not a deployed replacement, not
a production rollback, not a monitor-quality result, and not a support-state
transition.

## Failure Modes

Regression deletion is the classic false improvement. The candidate moves the
frontier while forgetting what the system already knew.

Rollback impossibility turns a bad canary into permanent damage. The old
artifact is gone, the state migration cannot be reversed, or no one owns the
recovery path.

Self-judged replacement lets the component being replaced propose, evaluate,
and approve its own successor.

Rollback theater is the paperwork version of safety. The record contains a
rollback field, but no executable path, state-compatibility check, trigger
condition, dry-run status, or owner exists when the monitor window fails.

Frontier bias makes the impressive gains visible and the quiet losses
invisible. The replacement looks better on new tasks while weakening rights
preservation, cost discipline, context boundaries, reproducibility, audit
retention, or human approval behavior.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a `replacement_transaction` schema and a
public-safe fixture suite. The fixture should show a proposal, shadow run,
canary with regression floor, failed rollback dry run that blocks default
promotion, and a separate reversible candidate with a valid rollback receipt.

The existing local harnesses are useful but narrow. Protocol validation checks
record shape. The capability replacement harness validates synthetic
replacement-transaction fixtures for field identity, qualification evidence,
regression results, non-widening authority checks, evaluator separation,
residual escrow, rollback receipts, approvals, monitor state, promotion
blockers, and non-claim boundaries. The readiness/residual gate harness checks
synthetic failed-regression and rollback-dry-run blockers.

Those checks do not prove deployed replacement behavior, runtime route quality,
real regression-suite quality, evaluator integrity, authority enforcement,
monitor-window success, rollback execution, or source interpretation.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a transaction control plane for improvement. Every
candidate implementation enters as a governed transaction with prior artifact,
candidate artifact, field identity, authority ceiling, regression floor,
evaluator boundary, canary scope, monitor window, rollback receipt, and
residual inheritance.

In that end state, capabilities can improve continuously without making the
system forget what it used to know. Regressions follow the field forward.
Failed canaries become residuals. Rollbacks are rehearsed and owned. State
migration is classified as reversible, compensatable, forked, or residual. A
default route is never just the latest winner; it is the candidate that
survived the transaction while preserving the floor.

Beyond current practice, replacement becomes compositional. Security,
governance rights, runtime adapters, benchmark ratchets, and self-improvement
gates all read the same transaction record. If a candidate changes audit
retention, handle scope, appeal paths, verification budget, or rollback
ownership, the transaction surfaces that as a boundary delta before promotion.

This remains a design destination. The book does not claim a deployed
replacement pipeline until replayable replacement traces, regression-
preservation tests, rollback dry runs, monitor-window records, governance
receipts, and failed-candidate examples exercise the transaction under
ordinary success and failure pressure.

The intent-governed replacement bridge adds a narrow but important check: a
replacement request must still point back to the command contract that gave it
authority. The synthetic bridge preserves the intent reference, command
reference, authority ceiling, stop conditions, evidence requirements, canary
scope, monitor window, rollback owner, and residuals. It also blocks a default
replacement request when the required approval receipt is missing. This is not
a deployed replacement, not production rollback, and not a support-state
transition.

## Summary

Capability Replacement and Rollback turns improvement into an accountable
state change. It keeps the prior artifact, candidate evidence, regression
floor, authority boundary, residuals, approvals, monitor window, and rollback
path visible while a candidate earns promotion.

The governing discipline is floor preservation. The frontier may move only
when the floor remains visible and recoverable.

## Handoff

Replacement becomes dangerous when it moves secrets, permissions, or privileged
context across a boundary the old capability was never allowed to cross.
Security Kernel and Digital SCIFs follows because rollback and qualification
are not enough if authority-bearing material can leak through logs, prompts,
summaries, memory, or tool adapters. Protected power must become handle-bound,
auditable, and absent by default.
